text: This is a rare case of a sciatic hernia managed with robotic mesh repair.
The patient is a sixty-six year old female with an incidental finding of a right-sided sciatic hernia on an MRI done for refractory diarrhea.

Her history was relevant for multiple surgeries including a hysterectomy, a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, two diagnostic laparoscopies, and an exploratory laparotomy with ileocolectomy for small bowel obstructions.
This slide shows the patient's MRI in which we can appreciate a protrusion of abdominal contents through the sciatic foramen.
It's important to remind ourselves of the relevant anatomy pertinent to sciatic hernias. In this representation, we can see a clear correlation with the ureter as well as the iliac vessels.
The surgeon begins the procedure by performing lysis of adhesions using sharp dissection, carefully separating the adhered tissues.

The surgeon identifies and exposes the right sciatic hernia defect, measuring approximately one by one centimeters in diameter, with the right ovary and right fallopian tube visible in close anatomical relation to the defect.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter positioned inferior and medial to the sciatic hernia defect, while the right iliac vessels are visible lateral and superior to the defect.
The surgeon inspects the left side, revealing no visible evidence of a sciatic hernia. Additionally, there is no visual indication of inguinal hernias on either side.
The surgeon begins repairing the sciatic hernia by creating a peritoneal flap to access the preperitoneal space, revealing the hernia defect and surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon completes the incision in the peritoneum, providing access to the preperitoneal space for further exploration and repair of the hernia defect.
The surgeon dissects the preperitoneal space to create a pocket for mesh placement, carefully avoiding injury to the visible right ureter and iliac vessels during the procedure.
During the dissection, the right sciatic nerve is identified, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, revealing that it is significantly deeper, measuring approximately two to three centimeters, compared to its diameter.
The surgeon places a single stitch to approximate the preperitoneal fat, providing protection for the right sciatic nerve and ureter from the mesh that will be placed inside the preperitoneal pocket.
The surgeon places a three by three centimeter piece of large pore monofilament soft mesh inside the peritoneal pocket, which was previously dissected to accommodate the mesh.
The surgeon ensures that the mesh is well positioned, lying flat and large enough to cover the entire defect, with the peritoneal flap completely covering it.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to close the peritoneal flap, securing the mesh in place and incorporating the hernia sac into the peritoneal closure.

Our patient had no postoperative complications. She was discharged home on the same day of the surgery and was found to be doing well on her follow-up in clinic after six weeks. Interestingly, she had had no more episodes of diarrhea after the surgery.

Sciatic hernias are rare conditions and therefore it's not surprising that most of the literature available on the topic consists of small case series or case reports. This paper found only ninety-nine reported cases of sciatic hernia in a span of a hundred and eight years. Only four percent had bilateral sciatic hernias. and there was no evidence of correlation with prior pelvic surgeries such as hysterectomies as seen in our patient. The presentation varied and it's unclear whether or not the diarrhea in our patient could be related in any way to the sciatic hernia. Her previous episodes of small bowel obstructions also may or may not be related.

Looking at the management of sciatic hernia, we can see a transition between a transgluteal approach in the early nineteen hundreds to a predominantly abdominal approach in more recent years. We can also see laparoscopic surgery coming into scene about twenty years ago, and now robotic surgery as well.
